comment: Premise-level source/provenance audit. Each audit-premise from Assumptions.lean is judged individually; group-level approval is not sufficient.

assumption_equal_cardinality.reason: Roth explicitly defines the marriage problem as the representative strict matching model, with equal numbers of men and women and one-to-one outcomes.

hcard : Fintype.card M = Fintype.card W.source_location: Roth82StableMatching.txt, lines 110-142: the marriage problem has sets M and W indexed by the same n, and an outcome is an invertible function M -> W; lines 176-180: Roth says the marriage problem was defined as having equal numbers of men and women.
hcard : Fintype.card M = Fintype.card W.reason: The Lean cardinality premise is the finite-type rendering of the paper's equal-size one-to-one marriage-problem domain.

assumption_simple_report_ranks_partner_f.reason: Roth defines the equivalent simple misrepresentation by placing the obtained partner first in the reported preference order before Lemma 1.

hfirst : ∀ w, w ≠ wstar → simple_report_.source_location: Roth82StableMatching.txt, lines 522-539: the simple misrepresentation P'' has y(mi) as mi's most preferred match, i.e. y(mi) P''(mi) wj for all wj != y(mi).
hfirst : ∀ w, w ≠ wstar → simple_report_.reason: The Lean premise states exactly that the simplified report ranks the obtained partner `wstar` strictly above every other woman, using lower numeric rank for higher preference.
